message.content:
I need to evaluate which item better reduces time-to-first-success for a new user getting a meaningful successful run.

**Item A (when_not_to_use_cardinal):** A section explaining when NOT to use the system. This is valuable for avoiding wasted effort, but it doesn't directly help a new user get a successful run. It's more of a "save time by not starting" guide rather than "get to success faster." A new user might benefit from knowing if they're in the wrong use case, but this doesn't guide them through a successful execution.

**Item B (public_api_recipe):** A minimal library example that constructs a MultiRerankRequest, installs a model policy, runs one attribute, and emits a report. This is a direct, hands-on code recipe. A new user can copy-paste this, run it, and immediately see output. This is the classic "quickstart" pattern that directly reduces time-to-first-success by providing a complete working example.

Item B is clearly more aligned with reducing time-to-first-success. A working code example that covers the full pipeline (construct → install → run → report) gives a new user an immediate path to a meaningful result. Item A, while useful, is more of a decision-making aid that doesn't produce a successful run.

The gap is substantial — B directly enables a first run while A doesn't contribute to execution at all. I'd estimate B is roughly 8-10x more impactful for this specific attribute.

```json
{"higher_id":"B","ratio":9.0,"confidence":0.82}
```
